title: Low Back Pain — Mechanical
oneLiner: Mechanical low back pain: a common, benign musculoskeletal pain (lumbar strain, facet joint, muscle) in working-age adults; self-limiting over days–weeks but causes significant disability and time off work.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Cauda Equina Syndrome (CES):** the single finding saying "this is the killer" = **acute urinary retention** (bladder distended on palpation, inability to void) OR **saddle anaesthesia** (loss of perineal sensation). Both are emergencies requiring immediate neurosurgical referral.
- **Spinal infection (discitis, osteomyelitis, epidural abscess):** **fever + severe constant back pain (worse at night, no mechanical component)** — especially in IVDU, immunocompromised, recent spinal surgery. Need urgent MRI + septic screen.
- **Malignancy (primary or metastatic):** **history of cancer + night pain** (wakes from sleep) + unremitting pain not eased by rest. Same red flag as infection but without fever — urges early imaging and cancer workup.
- **Vertebral fracture (osteoporotic or traumatic):** **sudden onset of severe pain after a fall or minimal trauma (e.g. lifting)** in elderly or known osteoporosis. Suspect on history; X-ray or CT confirms.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Dull, aching pain localised to lumbosacral region (L4–S1 most common)
  - Worse with bending, lifting, twisting; relieved by lying down or changing position
  - No radiation below the knee (if pain radiates below knee, think radiculopathy not pure mechanical)
  - Onset often after a specific activity (e.g. gardening, lifting, awkward movement) but can be insidious
  - Stiffness in morning, ease with movement (inflammatory back pain is opposite — morning stiffness >30 min, improves with exercise)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Normal vital signs (afebrile, no tachypnoea)
  - Full range of lumbar motion limited by pain — pain on flexion (typical) or extension (facet joint)
  - No neurological deficit (power, sensation, reflexes normal)
  - Straight leg raise (SLR) negative for nerve root tension (pain in back only, no leg pain); if positive below 70° with leg pain, suggests radiculopathy
  - Palpation: tender over paraspinal muscles, not over spinous processes (spinous tenderness = red flag)
  - No saddle anaesthesia, normal anal tone, normal perineal sensation
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Most likely → less likely:**
  - **Mechanical low back pain** — pain with movement, no leg symptoms, normal neuro exam
  - **Lumbar radiculopathy (e.g. disc herniation)** — unilateral leg pain (dermatomal) ± sensory loss, reflex changes; SLR positive (pain down leg <70°)
  - **Spinal stenosis** — bilateral leg symptoms (pseudoclaudication: worse walking, eased by sitting/forward flexion); affects elderly
  - **Inflammatory back pain (e.g. ankylosing spondylitis)** — morning stiffness >30 min, improves with exercise, onset <40 years, buttock pain alternating; HLA-B27 positive
  - **Non-mechanical causes (red flags)** — malignancy, infection, fracture, cauda equina (see Killers)
- **Splitter:** **SLR test** — if pain reproduces leg pain (not just back), it's radiculopathy; if only back pain, it's mechanical.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
  - Full neurological exam: L2–S1 myotomes, dermatomes, reflexes (patellar L4, Achilles S1), perianal sensation, anal tone
  - Straight leg raise (active and passive)
  - Bladder ultrasound for post-void residual if any bladder symptoms
  - Urinalysis to exclude UTI (can mimic or coexist)
- **Bloods:**
  - **Only if red flags present** (fever, night pain, weight loss, age <20/>55, trauma, cancer history): FBC, CRP, ESR, bone profile, PSA (if male >50), serum electrophoresis if myeloma suspected
  - No routine bloods for mechanical low back pain
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** **None** — NICE recommends no imaging for mechanical low back pain within first 4–6 weeks unless red flags
  - **If red flags:**
    - X-ray lumbar spine (fracture, spondylolisthesis, malignancy)
    - MRI lumbosacral spine (infection, cauda equina, malignancy, disc herniation) — urgent if CES suspected
    - CT if MRI contraindicated
  - **Second-line:** MRI or CT myelogram if surgery considered
- **Special tests:** Schober’s test for lumbar mobility (reduced in inflammatory back pain)
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:** NICE 2016 low back pain and sciatica: mechanical back pain defined by absence of red flags; no specific scoring system needed
- **Never-miss test:** **MRI spine (whole lumbosacral)** if any symptom of cauda equina or infection — omission means irreversible neurological deficit or death from sepsis.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Reassure: “most back pain resolves in days–weeks even without treatment”
  - Advise **stay active, not bed rest** (bed rest >2 days worsens outcomes)
  - Simple analgesia: paracetamol 1g PO (max 4g/day) and/or ibuprofen 400mg PO TDS with food (short course, max 3–5 days) — avoid opioids unless severe acute episode
  - Heat therapy (hot pack) for muscle spasm
  - If cauda equina suspected: stat MRI, call neurosurgery, placement of urinary catheter if retention
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - Rarely admitted unless for pain control (IV analgesia, NCA) or red flag workup
  - Encourage mobilisation with physiotherapy (early walking, gentle stretches)
  - Continue simple analgesia, consider addition of codeine 30–60mg PO QDS PRN (if step-up needed, but avoid prolonged use)
  - If admitted for red flag: initiate workup (MRI, bloods, bone scan if needed)
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Physiotherapy (manual therapy, exercise, postural advice)
  - Consider acupuncture/massage if no response to initial care
  - TENS machine may help
  - If pain persists >6 weeks: reassess, consider referral to spinal triage clinic (further imaging, injections)
  - Return to work advice: gradual, with modifications; not off-work for >2 weeks unless necessary
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **Fluids:** nil specific — encourage oral fluids
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** if prolonged immobility (e.g. fracture, cauda equina): low molecular weight heparin (e.g. enoxaparin 40mg SC OD [check local policy])
  - **Diet/NBM:** normal diet unless symptomatic
  - **Observation frequency:** NEWS2 Q4H if stable; if red flags, more frequent (1–2 hourly)
  - **Activity:** mobilise as tolerated, no strict bed rest
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents:**
  - Paracetamol: 1g PO/IV QDS (max 4g/day) — use IV if unable to swallow or acute pain
  - Ibuprofen: 400mg PO TDS (max 1.2g/day) — short course (3–7 days); increase to 600mg TDS if efficacy unclear
  - TENS machine (non-pharmacological)
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Diclofenac: 50mg PO TDS (or 75mg IM if acute severe) — use with PPI (e.g. omeprazole 20mg OD) if >75 years, prior GI bleed, or comedication with steroid/anticoagulant
  - Codeine phosphate: 30–60mg PO QDS PRN — avoid >7 days; never use in children <12 years
  - Weak opioids (tramadol 50–100mg PO QDS PRN) — only if NSAIDs + paracetamol fail; caution with SSRI/SNRI (serotonin syndrome)
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - NSAIDs: avoid if GFR <30, active GI bleed, uncontrolled hypertension, or asthma; use with PPI in at-risk
  - Codeine: avoid if severe respiratory disease, acute CVA, or concurrent CNS depressants
  - Tramadol: contraindicated in epilepsy, not for monotherapy in severe pain without specialist advice; increase risk of hyponatraemia with SSRIs
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - NSAIDs: avoid if eGFR <30; reduce dose if eGFR 30–59; [check local policy]
  - Paracetamol: safe in renal/hepatic impairment (max 3g/day if severe liver disease)
  - Codeine: avoid in severe renal impairment (accumulation of active metabolite)
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **NSAIDs in acute kidney injury** — prescribing ibuprofen 400mg TDS without checking creatinine in elderly or dehydrated patient (common cause of AKI on medical wards)
  - **Opioids for acute mechanical back pain** — starting codeine 60mg QDS without plan for duration leads to chronic use and side effects
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Rare with correct identification; misdiagnosis of red flag (CES, infection) leads to permanent neurological deficit or sepsis
  - Chronic pain syndrome (development of long-term pain if not managed early with activity)
- **Chronic:**
  - Disability, work loss (commonest cause of years lived with disability globally)
  - Depression/anxiety from chronic pain
  - Deconditioning, obesity, muscle wasting due to prolonged inactivity
sections.Prognosis: - Good: 80% improve within 6 weeks regardless of treatment; 90% improve within 12 weeks. Recurrence common (up to 50% within 1 year). Chronic pain (>12 weeks) develops in 5–10%, requiring multidisciplinary pain management.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **DO tell patients: “Bed rest is the enemy — mild discomfort is acceptable; keep moving within pain limits.”** This prevents deconditioning.
- **DO check for red flags at every appointment** (even if first visit clear); many are not present initially.
- **DO use heat therapy as first-line non-pharmacological** — cheap, evidence-supported, patient-loved.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **NOT performing a perianal exam and checking bladder residual** in a patient with bilateral leg symptoms or saddle paraesthesiae — missing cauda equina is a medicolegal disaster.
- **NOT prescribing a PPI with NSAIDs** in elderly patients (age >75) — leads to GI bleeds on the ward.
- **NOT differentiating mechanical from radiculopathy** — sending a mechanical back pain patient for an MRI that shows incidental disc bulge (seen in 30% of asymptomatic people) causes unnecessary worry and referrals.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
  - New leg weakness (foot drop, inability to walk on heels/toes)
  - Bladder or bowel dysfunction (inability to pass urine, incontinence)
  - Saddle numbness or loss of sensation in perineum
  - Fever, rigors, or severe night sweats
  - Worsening pain despite simple analgesia after 5 days
- **Follow-up:**
  - If not improved at 2 weeks: GP review (physio referral, consider imaging)
  - If worsening at any time: return to ED/A&E
  - If red flags absent: reassure and book outpatient physiotherapy within 4–6 weeks
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior immediately if:**
  - Suspected cauda equina syndrome: **NEWS2 >5** (fever, hypotension) — or any evidence of acute urinary retention (post-void residual >200mL) + saddle anaesthesia
  - Progressive bilateral leg weakness (unable to weight bear) — needs MRI + neurosurgery
  - Suspected spinal infection: **NEWS2 >3** + severe back pain + pyrexia — need septic workup + urgent MRI
  - Lactate >2 mmol/l with sepsis signs (infection red flag)
  - GCS <15 (rare, but can signify spinal shock or sepsis)
- **Call ICU if:**
  - Septicaemia from spinal infection requiring vasopressors
  - Post-surgical complication (haematoma, meningitis) after decompression

rawMarkdown:
## Low Back Pain — Mechanical

**One-liner:** Mechanical low back pain: a common, benign musculoskeletal pain (lumbar strain, facet joint, muscle) in working-age adults; self-limiting over days–weeks but causes significant disability and time off work.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Cauda Equina Syndrome (CES):** the single finding saying "this is the killer" = **acute urinary retention** (bladder distended on palpation, inability to void) OR **saddle anaesthesia** (loss of perineal sensation). Both are emergencies requiring immediate neurosurgical referral.
- **Spinal infection (discitis, osteomyelitis, epidural abscess):** **fever + severe constant back pain (worse at night, no mechanical component)** — especially in IVDU, immunocompromised, recent spinal surgery. Need urgent MRI + septic screen.
- **Malignancy (primary or metastatic):** **history of cancer + night pain** (wakes from sleep) + unremitting pain not eased by rest. Same red flag as infection but without fever — urges early imaging and cancer workup.
- **Vertebral fracture (osteoporotic or traumatic):** **sudden onset of severe pain after a fall or minimal trauma (e.g. lifting)** in elderly or known osteoporosis. Suspect on history; X-ray or CT confirms.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Dull, aching pain localised to lumbosacral region (L4–S1 most common)
  - Worse with bending, lifting, twisting; relieved by lying down or changing position
  - No radiation below the knee (if pain radiates below knee, think radiculopathy not pure mechanical)
  - Onset often after a specific activity (e.g. gardening, lifting, awkward movement) but can be insidious
  - Stiffness in morning, ease with movement (inflammatory back pain is opposite — morning stiffness >30 min, improves with exercise)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Normal vital signs (afebrile, no tachypnoea)
  - Full range of lumbar motion limited by pain — pain on flexion (typical) or extension (facet joint)
  - No neurological deficit (power, sensation, reflexes normal)
  - Straight leg raise (SLR) negative for nerve root tension (pain in back only, no leg pain); if positive below 70° with leg pain, suggests radiculopathy
  - Palpation: tender over paraspinal muscles, not over spinous processes (spinous tenderness = red flag)
  - No saddle anaesthesia, normal anal tone, normal perineal sensation

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Most likely → less likely:**
  - **Mechanical low back pain** — pain with movement, no leg symptoms, normal neuro exam
  - **Lumbar radiculopathy (e.g. disc herniation)** — unilateral leg pain (dermatomal) ± sensory loss, reflex changes; SLR positive (pain down leg <70°)
  - **Spinal stenosis** — bilateral leg symptoms (pseudoclaudication: worse walking, eased by sitting/forward flexion); affects elderly
  - **Inflammatory back pain (e.g. ankylosing spondylitis)** — morning stiffness >30 min, improves with exercise, onset <40 years, buttock pain alternating; HLA-B27 positive
  - **Non-mechanical causes (red flags)** — malignancy, infection, fracture, cauda equina (see Killers)
- **Splitter:** **SLR test** — if pain reproduces leg pain (not just back), it's radiculopathy; if only back pain, it's mechanical.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**
  - Full neurological exam: L2–S1 myotomes, dermatomes, reflexes (patellar L4, Achilles S1), perianal sensation, anal tone
  - Straight leg raise (active and passive)
  - Bladder ultrasound for post-void residual if any bladder symptoms
  - Urinalysis to exclude UTI (can mimic or coexist)
- **Bloods:**
  - **Only if red flags present** (fever, night pain, weight loss, age <20/>55, trauma, cancer history): FBC, CRP, ESR, bone profile, PSA (if male >50), serum electrophoresis if myeloma suspected
  - No routine bloods for mechanical low back pain
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** **None** — NICE recommends no imaging for mechanical low back pain within first 4–6 weeks unless red flags
  - **If red flags:**
    - X-ray lumbar spine (fracture, spondylolisthesis, malignancy)
    - MRI lumbosacral spine (infection, cauda equina, malignancy, disc herniation) — urgent if CES suspected
    - CT if MRI contraindicated
  - **Second-line:** MRI or CT myelogram if surgery considered
- **Special tests:** Schober’s test for lumbar mobility (reduced in inflammatory back pain)
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:** NICE 2016 low back pain and sciatica: mechanical back pain defined by absence of red flags; no specific scoring system needed
- **Never-miss test:** **MRI spine (whole lumbosacral)** if any symptom of cauda equina or infection — omission means irreversible neurological deficit or death from sepsis.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Reassure: “most back pain resolves in days–weeks even without treatment”
  - Advise **stay active, not bed rest** (bed rest >2 days worsens outcomes)
  - Simple analgesia: paracetamol 1g PO (max 4g/day) and/or ibuprofen 400mg PO TDS with food (short course, max 3–5 days) — avoid opioids unless severe acute episode
  - Heat therapy (hot pack) for muscle spasm
  - If cauda equina suspected: stat MRI, call neurosurgery, placement of urinary catheter if retention
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - Rarely admitted unless for pain control (IV analgesia, NCA) or red flag workup
  - Encourage mobilisation with physiotherapy (early walking, gentle stretches)
  - Continue simple analgesia, consider addition of codeine 30–60mg PO QDS PRN (if step-up needed, but avoid prolonged use)
  - If admitted for red flag: initiate workup (MRI, bloods, bone scan if needed)
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Physiotherapy (manual therapy, exercise, postural advice)
  - Consider acupuncture/massage if no response to initial care
  - TENS machine may help
  - If pain persists >6 weeks: reassess, consider referral to spinal triage clinic (further imaging, injections)
  - Return to work advice: gradual, with modifications; not off-work for >2 weeks unless necessary
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **Fluids:** nil specific — encourage oral fluids
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** if prolonged immobility (e.g. fracture, cauda equina): low molecular weight heparin (e.g. enoxaparin 40mg SC OD [check local policy])
  - **Diet/NBM:** normal diet unless symptomatic
  - **Observation frequency:** NEWS2 Q4H if stable; if red flags, more frequent (1–2 hourly)
  - **Activity:** mobilise as tolerated, no strict bed rest

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents:**
  - Paracetamol: 1g PO/IV QDS (max 4g/day) — use IV if unable to swallow or acute pain
  - Ibuprofen: 400mg PO TDS (max 1.2g/day) — short course (3–7 days); increase to 600mg TDS if efficacy unclear
  - TENS machine (non-pharmacological)
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Diclofenac: 50mg PO TDS (or 75mg IM if acute severe) — use with PPI (e.g. omeprazole 20mg OD) if >75 years, prior GI bleed, or comedication with steroid/anticoagulant
  - Codeine phosphate: 30–60mg PO QDS PRN — avoid >7 days; never use in children <12 years
  - Weak opioids (tramadol 50–100mg PO QDS PRN) — only if NSAIDs + paracetamol fail; caution with SSRI/SNRI (serotonin syndrome)
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - NSAIDs: avoid if GFR <30, active GI bleed, uncontrolled hypertension, or asthma; use with PPI in at-risk
  - Codeine: avoid if severe respiratory disease, acute CVA, or concurrent CNS depressants
  - Tramadol: contraindicated in epilepsy, not for monotherapy in severe pain without specialist advice; increase risk of hyponatraemia with SSRIs
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - NSAIDs: avoid if eGFR <30; reduce dose if eGFR 30–59; [check local policy]
  - Paracetamol: safe in renal/hepatic impairment (max 3g/day if severe liver disease)
  - Codeine: avoid in severe renal impairment (accumulation of active metabolite)
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **NSAIDs in acute kidney injury** — prescribing ibuprofen 400mg TDS without checking creatinine in elderly or dehydrated patient (common cause of AKI on medical wards)
  - **Opioids for acute mechanical back pain** — starting codeine 60mg QDS without plan for duration leads to chronic use and side effects

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Rare with correct identification; misdiagnosis of red flag (CES, infection) leads to permanent neurological deficit or sepsis
  - Chronic pain syndrome (development of long-term pain if not managed early with activity)
- **Chronic:**
  - Disability, work loss (commonest cause of years lived with disability globally)
  - Depression/anxiety from chronic pain
  - Deconditioning, obesity, muscle wasting due to prolonged inactivity

### Prognosis
- Good: 80% improve within 6 weeks regardless of treatment; 90% improve within 12 weeks. Recurrence common (up to 50% within 1 year). Chronic pain (>12 weeks) develops in 5–10%, requiring multidisciplinary pain management.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **DO tell patients: “Bed rest is the enemy — mild discomfort is acceptable; keep moving within pain limits.”** This prevents deconditioning.
- **DO check for red flags at every appointment** (even if first visit clear); many are not present initially.
- **DO use heat therapy as first-line non-pharmacological** — cheap, evidence-supported, patient-loved.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **NOT performing a perianal exam and checking bladder residual** in a patient with bilateral leg symptoms or saddle paraesthesiae — missing cauda equina is a medicolegal disaster.
- **NOT prescribing a PPI with NSAIDs** in elderly patients (age >75) — leads to GI bleeds on the ward.
- **NOT differentiating mechanical from radiculopathy** — sending a mechanical back pain patient for an MRI that shows incidental disc bulge (seen in 30% of asymptomatic people) causes unnecessary worry and referrals.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**
  - New leg weakness (foot drop, inability to walk on heels/toes)
  - Bladder or bowel dysfunction (inability to pass urine, incontinence)
  - Saddle numbness or loss of sensation in perineum
  - Fever, rigors, or severe night sweats
  - Worsening pain despite simple analgesia after 5 days
- **Follow-up:**
  - If not improved at 2 weeks: GP review (physio referral, consider imaging)
  - If worsening at any time: return to ED/A&E
  - If red flags absent: reassure and book outpatient physiotherapy within 4–6 weeks

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior immediately if:**
  - Suspected cauda equina syndrome: **NEWS2 >5** (fever, hypotension) — or any evidence of acute urinary retention (post-void residual >200mL) + saddle anaesthesia
  - Progressive bilateral leg weakness (unable to weight bear) — needs MRI + neurosurgery
  - Suspected spinal infection: **NEWS2 >3** + severe back pain + pyrexia — need septic workup + urgent MRI
  - Lactate >2 mmol/l with sepsis signs (infection red flag)
  - GCS <15 (rare, but can signify spinal shock or sepsis)
- **Call ICU if:**
  - Septicaemia from spinal infection requiring vasopressors
  - Post-surgical complication (haematoma, meningitis) after decompression
